validation.comparison_result: Q6 results match ground truth (rows_expected: 1, rows_actual: 1, match: true)

profiling.notes: perf not available; however time -v shows 1876% CPU utilization, minimal page faults (0 major, 25887 minor), good parallel scaling

Q6.notes: Average of 5 runs: 28.09, 28.77, 28.97, 29.54, 30.20 ms. Tight variance ~2 ms. Correct result: 1230113636.01

Q6.bottleneck: CPU-bound: lack of SIMD vectorization for predicate evaluation and aggregation; zone map index not utilized despite availability; potential to squeeze more parallelism
Q6.root_cause: Current implementation: (1) uses scalar comparisons instead of SIMD (4-8x speedup potential with AVX2/AVX-512 on 64-core system with AVX-512 support), (2) zone map on l_shipdate defined in storage but not loaded/used in code, (3) predicate evaluation order not optimized for selectivity, (4) no explicit SIMD reduction for partial sums across SIMD registers

analysis.overall: Q6 executes correctly and completes in 29.3 ms on average. Compared to DuckDB (19.2-22.1 ms), there is a 7-10 ms gap (33-50% slower). Gap to GenDB target (14.19-19 ms) is 10-15 ms. The code already implements several best practices: (1) morsel-driven parallelism with 48 threads, (2) thread-local aggregation avoiding locks, (3) fused filter+aggregation in single loop, (4) MADV_SEQUENTIAL for HDD access pattern. The main optimization opportunities are: (1) SIMD vectorization of predicates and horizontal reduction, (2) zone map skipping, (3) predicate reordering by selectivity, (4) SIMD-friendly data layout alignment.

performance_optimizations.description: Replace scalar predicate evaluation (lines 118-120) with SIMD batch processing. Use AVX-512 (available on hardware) to evaluate 8 rows in parallel: __m512i shipdate_vec = _mm512_loadu_si512(shipdate+i); __m512i cmp = _mm512_and_si512(_mm512_cmpge_epi32_mask(shipdate_vec, DATE_1994_01_01), ...). Process discount/quantity similarly. Accumulate partial sums in SIMD registers using horizontal adds, then reduce to scalar at loop end. Requires: alignment to 64-byte boundaries for mmap'd columns (may need realignment in mmap), handling tail rows with scalar loop.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-35% wall-clock time reduction (6-10 ms) based on: (1) 8x throughput on predicate evaluation with AVX-512, (2) reduced branch mispredictions with branchless SIMD comparisons, (3) better IPC via SIMD pipelining. Conservative estimate: 4-8 rows processed per cycle instead of 1, applied to 60M row dataset.
performance_optimizations.risk: medium - requires SIMD intrinsics which are platform-specific; must handle alignment for mmap'd columns; tail-row handling adds complexity; AVX-512 instruction generation depends on compiler flags (-mavx512f -mavx512dq)

performance_optimizations.description: Load zone maps metadata from storage (available at lineitem.l_shipdate_zone_map per storage_design.json line 28). Zone maps store min/max per block (likely 150K rows per block given block_size: 150000). Before processing each morsel, check if the morsel's block zone (shipdate_min, shipdate_max) overlaps the filter range [DATE_1994_01_01, DATE_1995_01_01). If no overlap, skip the block entirely with continue. Apply similar logic for discount (0.05-0.07) if zone maps available. Implementation: read zone_map metadata, build in-memory lookup table indexed by block ID, check before inner loop.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-40% reduction in data scanned (depending on data distribution) based on: (1) lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate (storage_design.json line 24), (2) 1994 calendar year filter has ~27% selectivity historically in TPC-H, (3) zone maps can skip 70%+ of 1995+ blocks, (4) estimated ~4-6 ms savings if 40% of data is skipped. Real-world: depends on actual min/max distribution in gendb files.
performance_optimizations.risk: low-medium - zone map format must be determined from gendb storage; requires parsing or reverse-engineering metadata; if zone maps unavailable or malformed, graceful fallback to full scan; metadata loading adds ~0.5-1 ms overhead

performance_optimizations.description: Reorder predicates in the main filter condition (lines 118-120) from current (shipdate, discount, quantity) to most selective first. Estimated selectivity: l_quantity < 24 is ~50% (random quantity 1-50), l_discount BETWEEN 0.05-0.07 is ~2% (narrow range, most discounts are 0 or discrete values), l_shipdate range is ~27% (1 year of 40+ years of data). Optimal order: discount (0.02) → shipdate (0.27) → quantity (0.50). This minimizes wasted comparisons via short-circuit evaluation. Code change: swap condition order in line 118.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% reduction in instruction count / branch mispredictions (~1-2 ms) based on: (1) discount check eliminates 98% of rows early, (2) fewer downstream comparisons executed, (3) better branch predictor behavior with fewer branches taken.
performance_optimizations.risk: low - simple code reordering, no new dependencies, minimal compilation impact; must verify selectivity assumptions against actual data distribution

performance_optimizations.description: Ensure l_discount and l_extendedprice columns are aligned to 64-byte cache-line boundaries in the mmap region. Current mmap loads columns sequentially but doesn't guarantee inter-column alignment. If l_quantity starts at a misaligned offset, first-access loads become slower. Solution: (1) check actual column offsets in gendb files, (2) if misaligned, apply manual padding during column loading, or (3) use _mm_prefetch() explicitly within the morsel loop at intervals. Add explicit prefetch: `_mm_prefetch(shipdate + i + 256, _MM_HINT_T1)` to pull L2 cache ahead of computation.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% reduction in cache stalls (~1-2 ms) based on: (1) removal of unaligned load penalties (4-8 cycle stalls), (2) explicit prefetching hides main-memory latency better than MADV_SEQUENTIAL alone. Conservative estimate for HDD (slower random access): 2-4 ms savings if column alignment is currently poor.
performance_optimizations.risk: low - prefetch hints are no-ops on modern CPUs if predictions are wrong; alignment changes are non-invasive if done in column loading code

performance_optimizations.technique: SIMD Horizontal Reduction for Partial Sums
performance_optimizations.description: Current code accumulates partial sums in a scalar double (line 102). With SIMD vectorization, each SIMD register lane accumulates a partial sum. At the end of the morsel loop, reduce all lanes to a single scalar using horizontal adds (e.g., __m512d::hadd() or _mm512_reduce_add_pd()). This avoids per-iteration scalar updates and leverages SIMD register bandwidth. Implementation: maintain 8x __m512d registers during loop, each accumulating product sums for disjoint rows; at morsel end, reduce to thread_local_sum.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-5% reduction in memory write stalls (~1 ms) based on: (1) reduces scalar store pressure, (2) allows better out-of-order execution across SIMD lanes, (3) fewer data dependencies in accumulation chain.
performance_optimizations.risk: low-medium - only viable with priority 1 (SIMD vectorization); requires careful SIMD lane management; compiler support for reduction intrinsics varies

summary: Q6 is **correct and fast** (29.3 ms, matching ground truth), but leaves significant performance on the table. Current implementation is 33-50% slower than DuckDB (gap: 7-10 ms) and 85-100% slower than GenDB target (gap: 10-15 ms). The code already implements strong parallelism (48 threads, morsel-driven), memory efficiency (column pruning, MADV_SEQUENTIAL), and fusion (single scan+filter+agg loop). **Primary bottleneck is CPU**: lack of SIMD vectorization on 64-core system with AVX-512 support. **Secondary bottleneck**: zone maps defined in storage but unused in query code, missing an opportunity for 15-40% block skipping on date-filtered data. **Recommended action path**: (1) **Immediate (P1)**: Add AVX-512 predicate vectorization → estimated 6-10 ms gain, (2) **Secondary (P2)**: Implement zone map skipping → estimated 4-6 ms gain, (3) **Tertiary (P3-5)**: predicate reordering, alignment/prefetch tuning → estimated 1-4 ms gain. Combined potential: **11-20 ms improvement** (37-67% speedup) to reach 9-18 ms range, competitive with DuckDB (19 ms).
